comments.author: @JackDaniels-l7m
comments.text:
I lived and worked in the Canadian Arctic for years, places like Cambridge Bay(the largesst community) to Tuktoyaktuk, Gjoa Haven, Spence Bay and Pelly bay to mention a few....some communities that had less than 200 people and most were Inuit...I never had the commodities and convenience that these people have...not the stores, gyms, movie theaters or any form of modern communication...the nights were equally long and cold...the Antarctic group have it so easy and comfortable by comparison....
That said....it was some of the most enjoyable times I lived....I would go the Antarctic in a heartbeat given the opportunity... Everyone should experience these places at least once in their life...

comments.author: @NLaura77
comments.text:
What I don’t understand is why everything looks so ugly. I know it’s hard to travel there and transport things, but they could still create a nicer environment. The interiors are ugly, the buildings are ugly, their work clothes, their plates , everything. Temu even sells beautiful cheap fake flowers ))) I’m just saying  even a tiny bit of decoration would make the interior more pleasant.
Honestly, everything looks like an industrial container yard, with those old military-style plates and equipment. And I do understand that there is no local industry here, and these are not real cities like the Russian towns in the north  but still, they could make things a little more aesthetic, just enough to make being there more enjoyable. A minimal sense of aesthetics is not a luxury, but a basic human need, especially in a place where you see nothing but whiteness and metal for months.
Right now the walls are disgusting, the furniture is ugly, everything is ugly. Russians also live in insanely cold places  there are several cities like that, Norilsk, Yakutsk, and many smaller ones  and nowhere is it like this. Transport is difficult there too, they don’t have their own produce, trees, etc. And still, they eat fruit and it’s not a luxury, and the buildings look nice, they care about everything as if they weren’t living in minus 60. I know that’s the Arctic and this is the Antarctic, but still, I don’t understand the lack of need to make it beautiful inside the buildings.

comments.text: I’ve been there twice, December and January. What struck me the most was not only the superb beauty but the silence. A silence ancient and almost heavy as though it is saying, “we don’t want you here!” The second time was on a small expedition ship and travel by zodiac to the mainland. I walked among the penguins and felt again the awesome silence.

comments.text: It is the most beautiful and fragile place on earth. We have to do everything we can to keep it safe and secure. Every minute we sailed was a photograph, every sunrise the most amazing I ever saw. If you ever get the chance please go. Nothing on earth is even remotely close to this place.
